speaking.intro_th: ฝึกพูดตามรูปแบบ IELTS Speaking — Part 1 ถาม-ตอบเรื่องตัวเอง · Part 2 พูดคนเดียว ~2 นาที · Part 3 ถกความเห็นเชิงลึก

part1.topic_th: การทำงานและการเรียน

qs.q: Do you work or are you a student?
qs.sample: At the moment I'm working full-time as a marketing assistant at a small company. I've been in this role for about two years now, and I really enjoy the mix of creative and analytical tasks it involves.

qs.q: Why did you choose your job or subject?
qs.sample: I chose marketing because I've always been fascinated by how brands connect with people. It also suits my personality, since I'm quite outgoing and I love coming up with new ideas.

qs.q: What do you like most about your work or studies?
qs.sample: What I like most is that no two days are ever the same. One day I might be writing content, and the next I'm analysing campaign results, so it keeps things fresh and interesting.

qs.q: Is there anything you would like to change about it?
qs.sample: If I could change one thing, I'd probably reduce the amount of routine paperwork we have to deal with. It can be a bit tedious and takes time away from the more creative side of the job.

qs.q: Where is your hometown?
qs.sample: My hometown is Chiang Mai, a city in the north of Thailand. It's surrounded by mountains and it's well known for its temples and its laid-back atmosphere.

qs.q: What do you like about your hometown?
qs.sample: The thing I love most is how relaxed the pace of life is compared to big cities. The people are really friendly, and you're never far from beautiful nature or a good local market.

qs.q: Is it a good place for young people to live?
qs.sample: I'd say it's a great place in many ways because it's affordable and safe. However, some young people move away for university or work, simply because there are more career opportunities in the capital.

qs.q: Has your hometown changed much in recent years?
qs.sample: Yes, it's changed quite a lot actually. A lot of new cafes and co-working spaces have opened up, and it's become much more popular with tourists and digital nomads than it used to be.

part1.topic_th: บ้านและที่พักอาศัย

qs.q: Do you live in a house or an apartment?
qs.sample: I currently live in a small apartment in the city centre. It's not huge, but it's cosy and it has everything I need, plus the location is really convenient for getting to work.

qs.q: What is your favourite room in your home?
qs.sample: My favourite room is definitely the living room because that's where I unwind after a long day. I've got a comfy sofa and plenty of natural light, so it feels really calming.

qs.q: Would you like to change anything about your home?
qs.sample: If I had the chance, I'd love to have a bigger balcony where I could grow some plants. Right now my space is quite limited, so a bit of greenery would make it feel more homely.

qs.q: What kind of home would you like to live in the future?
qs.sample: In the future, I'd love to own a house with a garden somewhere a little quieter. I imagine having an open kitchen and a study, so I'd have space to both entertain friends and work from home.

part1.topic_th: งานอดิเรกและเวลาว่าง

qs.q: What do you like to do in your free time?
qs.sample: In my free time I really enjoy reading and going for long walks. I find both of them relaxing, and they give me a nice break from staring at a screen all day.

qs.q: Do you have any hobbies you have had since childhood?
qs.sample: Yes, I've been drawing since I was a little kid. It started as just doodling in my notebooks, but over the years it's grown into a proper hobby that I'm quite passionate about.

qs.q: Do you prefer indoor or outdoor activities?
qs.sample: It really depends on my mood, but I'd say I lean towards outdoor activities. Being outside in the fresh air always lifts my spirits, whereas indoor hobbies can feel a bit isolating after a while.

qs.q: Is there a new hobby you would like to try?
qs.sample: I'd love to give pottery a go at some point. There's something really satisfying about making things with your hands, and I think it would be a great way to switch off and be creative.

part1.topic_th: อาหารและการทำอาหาร

qs.q: What is your favourite food?
qs.sample: My all-time favourite has to be a good bowl of noodle soup. I love how the flavours are rich and comforting, and it's the kind of dish I could happily eat any day of the week.

qs.q: Do you like cooking?
qs.sample: Yes, I really enjoy cooking, especially at the weekend when I have more time. I find it quite therapeutic, and there's nothing better than sitting down to a meal you've made from scratch.

qs.q: Do you prefer eating at home or in restaurants?
qs.sample: Most of the time I prefer eating at home because it's healthier and cheaper. That said, I do love going out to restaurants now and then, mainly to try new dishes I wouldn't cook myself.

qs.q: Has your diet changed compared to the past?
qs.sample: Yes, it has changed quite a bit. I've started eating a lot more vegetables and cutting back on fast food, mostly because I've become more conscious about staying healthy as I've got older.

part1.topic_th: สภาพอากาศและฤดูกาล

qs.q: What is the weather like in your country?
qs.sample: Thailand is generally hot and humid for most of the year. We don't really have four seasons; instead we have a hot season, a rainy season, and a cooler, drier season around the end of the year.

qs.q: What is your favourite season?
qs.sample: My favourite is definitely the cool season, which we get around December. The temperature drops a little and there's a fresh breeze, so it's the perfect time to be outside without feeling sweaty.

qs.q: Does the weather affect your mood?
qs.sample: Yes, it affects me quite a lot actually. On a bright, sunny day I feel energetic and motivated, but when it's grey and rainy I tend to feel a bit sluggish and just want to stay in.

qs.q: Do you prefer hot or cold weather?
qs.sample: I'd have to say I prefer cooler weather. Since it's hot for most of the year where I live, I really appreciate those cooler days, and I also find it easier to sleep when it's not so warm.

qs.q: How often do you use technology?
qs.sample: Honestly, I use technology pretty much all day long. From my phone in the morning to my laptop at work, it's become such a big part of my daily life that I can hardly imagine going without it.

qs.q: What is your favourite piece of technology?
qs.sample: My favourite gadget has to be my smartphone. It's incredibly versatile, since I use it for everything from staying in touch with friends to taking photos and even paying for things.

qs.q: Do you think people rely too much on technology?
qs.sample: I do think a lot of people, myself included, have become quite dependent on it. It's brilliant for convenience, but sometimes I worry we're losing the ability to do simple things without a device.

qs.q: How has technology changed the way you communicate?
qs.sample: It's changed things enormously. I can now video call my family instantly even when they're far away, whereas in the past we would have had to rely on letters or expensive phone calls.

part1.topic_th: กิจวัตรประจำวัน

qs.q: What does your typical day look like?
qs.sample: On a typical weekday I get up around seven, have a quick breakfast, and head to work. After a busy day at the office, I usually cook dinner, catch up on a series, and try to get to bed by eleven.

qs.q: Are you a morning person or a night person?
qs.sample: I'd definitely say I'm more of a morning person. I tend to feel most productive and clear-headed in the early hours, whereas by the evening I run out of energy quite quickly.

qs.q: Is your routine on weekends different from weekdays?
qs.sample: Yes, my weekends are a lot more relaxed. I usually allow myself a lie-in, and instead of rushing around I spend my time meeting friends or just pottering about at home.

qs.q: Would you like to change anything about your daily routine?
qs.sample: I'd love to build in more time for exercise. At the moment my schedule is quite packed, so I often skip the gym, and I know I'd feel much healthier if I could fit it in regularly.

part1.topic_th: ครอบครัวและเพื่อน

qs.q: Do you have a large family or a small family?
qs.sample: I'd say a fairly small one — it's just my parents, my younger sister and me. But we're quite close to our relatives, so big family dinners still happen a few times a year.

qs.q: How much time do you spend with your family?
qs.sample: Not as much as I'd like, to be honest, because of work. But we always have dinner together on Sundays, and I call my mum almost every day.

qs.q: Who is your best friend?
qs.sample: That would be Ploy — we've known each other since high school. She just gets me, and even if we don't talk for weeks, nothing feels awkward when we meet again.

qs.q: Do you prefer spending time with family or friends?
qs.sample: It really depends on my mood. With family I can completely relax and be myself, but with friends I tend to do more fun, spontaneous things like trying out new cafés.

qs.q: Is it easy to make friends where you live?
qs.sample: I think so, yes. Thai people are generally quite friendly and open, so striking up a conversation isn't hard. The tricky part is turning those casual chats into real friendships.

part1.topic_th: การช้อปปิ้ง

qs.q: Do you enjoy shopping?
qs.sample: Yes and no. I love browsing for clothes and gadgets, but I find grocery shopping a bit of a chore — it's just something I have to get done every week.

qs.q: Do you prefer shopping online or in physical stores?
qs.sample: Mostly online these days, because it's so convenient and often cheaper. But for things like shoes or clothes, I still prefer to go to a shop so I can try them on first.

qs.q: Who do you usually go shopping with?
qs.sample: Usually my sister, because she's brutally honest about what suits me. If I go alone, I tend to buy things on impulse and regret them later.

qs.q: What was the last thing you bought?
qs.sample: Actually, just yesterday I bought a new pair of running shoes online. My old ones were completely worn out, so it was more of a necessity than a treat.

qs.q: Do you think you spend too much money on shopping?
qs.sample: Sometimes, especially during the big online sales — those flash deals are hard to resist. But I've started setting myself a monthly budget, and it's helped me cut down quite a bit.

qs.q: What kind of music do you like?
qs.sample: I listen to a bit of everything, but mostly pop and acoustic stuff. Lately I've been really into Thai indie bands — their lyrics feel very honest and relatable.

qs.q: When do you usually listen to music?
qs.sample: Mainly during my commute and while I'm working out. Music makes the traffic in Bangkok much more bearable, and upbeat songs keep me going at the gym.

qs.q: Can you play any musical instruments?
qs.sample: I learned a little guitar when I was a teenager, but I've forgotten most of it. I can still play a few basic chords, and I'd love to pick it up again someday.

qs.q: Have you ever been to a live concert?
qs.sample: Yes, a couple of times. The last one was a music festival by the beach, and the atmosphere was incredible — hearing songs live hits so much harder than through headphones.

qs.q: Is music important in your culture?
qs.sample: Definitely. In Thailand, music is part of almost every celebration, from temple fairs to weddings. Traditional Thai music is still taught in schools, which I think is lovely.

part1.topic_th: กีฬาและการออกกำลังกาย

qs.q: Do you do any exercise regularly?
qs.sample: Yes, I try to jog two or three times a week in the park near my place. It's nothing intense, but it clears my head and helps me sleep better.

qs.q: What sports are popular in your country?
qs.sample: Football is huge — people stay up late just to watch the English Premier League. And of course there's Muay Thai, our traditional boxing, which is famous all over the world.

qs.q: Did you play any sports when you were a child?
qs.sample: I played badminton a lot with my dad after school. I wasn't particularly good, but those matches are some of my happiest childhood memories.

qs.q: Do you prefer watching sports or playing them?
qs.sample: Playing, for sure. Watching is fun with friends, but I get restless just sitting there. I'd much rather be moving around and breaking a sweat myself.

qs.q: Do you think exercise is important?
qs.sample: Absolutely. It's not just about staying in shape — I notice my mood and energy drop whenever I skip exercising for a week. It's like maintenance for both body and mind.

part1.topic_th: การท่องเที่ยวและวันหยุด

qs.q: Do you like travelling?
qs.sample: I love it — it's probably my favourite way to spend money. There's something exciting about waking up somewhere new and not knowing exactly what the day will bring.

qs.q: Where did you go on your last holiday?
qs.sample: I went to Chiang Mai in the north of Thailand with a few friends. We visited temples, ate amazing northern food, and the cool weather was a nice break from Bangkok.

qs.q: Do you prefer travelling alone or with other people?
qs.sample: With a small group, ideally two or three close friends. Travelling alone sounds peaceful, but I like having someone to share the experience with — and to split the taxi fare!

qs.q: Do you prefer the beach or the mountains?
qs.sample: The mountains, definitely. I grew up near the sea, so beaches feel a bit ordinary to me, whereas cool mountain air and morning mist still feel like a real escape.

qs.q: Is there a country you would like to visit in the future?
qs.sample: Japan is at the top of my list. I'd love to see the cherry blossoms in spring, and I'm a big fan of Japanese food, so I'd probably spend most of the trip eating.

part1.topic_th: สัตว์และสัตว์เลี้ยง

qs.q: Do you have a pet?
qs.sample: Yes, I have a cat called Mochi. She's quite independent and ignores me half the time, but she always sleeps at the end of my bed, which I secretly love.

qs.q: Did you have any pets when you were a child?
qs.sample: We had a dog named Kao — he was a street dog my dad adopted. He was incredibly loyal and used to wait at the gate for me to come home from school every day.

qs.q: Are pets popular in your country?
qs.sample: Very popular, especially in the last few years. A lot of people in Bangkok treat their pets like family members — there are even cafés and hotels just for dogs and cats.

qs.q: What's your favourite animal?
qs.sample: Elephants, without a doubt. They're the national animal of Thailand, and they're so intelligent and gentle. I visited an elephant sanctuary once and it was unforgettable.

qs.q: Do you think keeping pets is good for people?
qs.sample: Yes, definitely. Pets give people companionship and a sense of responsibility. My cat honestly makes coming home after a stressful day so much nicer.

part1.topic_th: การอ่านและหนังสือ

qs.q: Do you like reading?
qs.sample: Yes, though I go through phases. Some months I finish two or three books, and other times I barely touch one. Lately I've been reading more because I deleted a few apps from my phone.

qs.q: What kind of books do you enjoy?
qs.sample: Mostly novels, especially mysteries — I love trying to guess the ending before it's revealed. I also read the occasional self-development book when I need a bit of motivation.

qs.q: Do you prefer paper books or e-books?
qs.sample: E-books for convenience, since I can read on my phone anywhere. But nothing beats the feel of a real paper book — I still buy physical copies of my favourites.

qs.q: Did you read a lot when you were a child?
qs.sample: Quite a lot, actually. I was obsessed with Thai comic books and folk tales. My grandmother used to buy me one every time she went to the market.

qs.q: Do people in your country read much?
qs.sample: Honestly, probably not as much as they should — most people prefer scrolling on their phones. That said, book fairs in Bangkok are always packed, so there's clearly still a love for reading.

part1.topic_th: โซเชียลมีเดีย

qs.q: Which social media platforms do you use?
qs.sample: Mainly Instagram and LINE — LINE is basically essential in Thailand for chatting with family and colleagues. I also watch a lot of short videos on TikTok, probably more than I should.

qs.q: How much time do you spend on social media every day?
qs.sample: Probably around two hours, mostly in the evening. I've set a screen-time limit on my phone, but I have to admit I ignore the warning more often than not.

qs.q: What do you usually use social media for?
qs.sample: Mostly to keep up with friends and follow food and travel pages. I don't post much myself — I'm more of a silent scroller than a content creator.

qs.q: Do you think people spend too much time on social media?
qs.sample: Yes, myself included. It's designed to keep you hooked, so it's easy to lose an hour without even noticing. I think most of us could do with putting the phone down more often.

qs.q: Has social media changed the way people communicate?
qs.sample: Completely. People message instead of calling now, and even my grandparents send stickers on LINE. It keeps us connected, but face-to-face conversations feel rarer than they used to be.

part2.title: Describe a person who has influenced you
card: who this person is
how you know them
what they did that influenced you
and explain why they had such an impact on you
part2.model: I'd like to talk about my aunt, Malee, who has probably shaped me more than anyone else. She's my mother's younger sister, and I grew up spending almost every weekend at her house because my parents worked long hours. What really influenced me was the way she ran her own small bakery. She started with practically nothing, just a second-hand oven and a lot of determination, and over the years she built it into a business that supports the whole family. I remember watching her wake up at four in the morning without ever complaining. To be honest, that taught me the value of hard work far better than any lecture at school could. On top of that, she always encouraged me to be curious and to ask questions, which is something I still carry with me today. The reason she had such a huge impact is that she led by example rather than just telling me what to do. Whenever I feel like giving up on something, I think of her, and it pushes me to keep going. All in all, I owe a great deal of who I am to her.

phrases.en: she has shaped me more than anyone else
phrases.th: เธอหล่อหลอมฉันมากกว่าใครทั้งหมด
พูดตามตรงนะ
เธอทำให้ดูเป็นตัวอย่าง
โดยรวมแล้ว

part2.title: Describe a place you like to visit
card: where this place is
how often you go there
what you do there
and explain why you enjoy visiting it
part2.model: I'd like to describe a little seaside town called Hua Hin, which is about three hours south of Bangkok. I try to get down there at least a couple of times a year, usually whenever I need a proper break from the city. What I love most is that it's peaceful without being completely dead. Typically, I'll spend the mornings walking along the beach, and in the evenings I head to the night market to sample the seafood, which is absolutely delicious and surprisingly cheap. On top of that, there are some lovely little cafés tucked away in the backstreets where I can just sit and read for hours. The main reason I enjoy going there is that it lets me switch off completely. In the city I'm constantly checking my phone, but the moment I arrive in Hua Hin, I feel my shoulders relax. It's the kind of place that recharges my batteries. Honestly, if I could, I'd move there permanently. For now, though, it remains my go-to escape whenever life gets a bit too hectic.

phrases.en: whenever I need a proper break
phrases.th: เมื่อไหร่ที่ฉันต้องการพักผ่อนจริงๆ

phrases.en: it lets me switch off completely
phrases.th: มันทำให้ฉันปล่อยวางได้อย่างสมบูรณ์
มันช่วยชาร์จพลังให้ฉัน
ที่หนีเที่ยวประจำของฉัน

part2.title: Describe a possession that is important to you
card: what the object is
how you got it
how long you have had it
and explain why it is so important to you
part2.model: The possession I'd like to talk about is an old film camera that belonged to my grandfather. It's a heavy, silver Nikon from the 1970s, and it still works perfectly, which amazes me every time I use it. I inherited it about five years ago when my grandfather passed away, and my grandmother insisted that I should have it because I was the only one in the family who showed any interest in photography. I've cherished it ever since. What makes it so special isn't really the camera itself, but everything it represents. Whenever I hold it, I can almost picture my grandfather taking pictures at family gatherings, and in a way it keeps his memory alive. On a practical level, it's also forced me to slow down and think carefully before I take a shot, because film isn't cheap and you only get thirty-six frames. That's actually made me a better photographer. All things considered, it's not the most valuable thing I own in terms of money, but sentimentally it's absolutely priceless to me.

phrases.th: มันเคยเป็นของคุณตาฉัน
ฉันทะนุถนอมมันมาตลอด
มันทำให้ความทรงจำของเขายังคงอยู่
ในแง่ความรู้สึกมันมีค่าจนประเมินไม่ได้

part2.title: Describe an event that made you happy
card: what the event was
when and where it happened
who was there with you
and explain why it made you so happy
part2.model: I'd like to describe the day I graduated from university, which was easily one of the happiest days of my life. It took place two years ago at my university's main auditorium in Bangkok, on a scorching hot April morning. Almost my entire family came along, including my parents, my younger brother, and even my grandmother, who rarely travels these days because of her knees. The ceremony itself was quite long and, to be honest, a little tedious, but the moment I walked across the stage to receive my certificate, everything felt worth it. I could hear my brother cheering far too loudly from the back, which was slightly embarrassing but also really touching. Afterwards, we all went out for a big lunch to celebrate. The reason it made me so happy wasn't just the achievement itself; it was seeing how proud my parents were after everything they'd sacrificed to put me through school. In that moment, I felt like I'd finally paid them back in some small way. Looking back, it's a memory I'll treasure forever.

phrases.en: one of the happiest days of my life
phrases.th: หนึ่งในวันที่มีความสุขที่สุดในชีวิต
ทุกอย่างรู้สึกคุ้มค่า
มันน่าประทับใจมาก
ความทรงจำที่ฉันจะเก็บไว้ตลอดไป

part2.title: Describe a hobby or activity you enjoy
card: what the hobby is
how you got started with it
how often you do it
and explain why you enjoy it
part2.model: The hobby I'd like to talk about is rock climbing, which I picked up about three years ago. I actually got into it completely by accident. A friend of mine dragged me along to an indoor climbing gym for his birthday, and although I was terrible at first, I was instantly hooked. These days I try to go at least twice a week, usually after work when I need to burn off some stress. What I love about climbing is that it's both physical and mental at the same time. You're not just relying on strength; you have to think through each move almost like solving a puzzle. On top of that, there's an amazing sense of achievement when you finally reach the top of a route you've been struggling with for weeks. The community is another big draw, because everyone is so supportive and always cheering each other on. Honestly, it's completely transformed how I spend my free time. Before, I'd just scroll through my phone in the evenings, but now I have something that keeps me fit and clears my head. I can't imagine giving it up.

phrases.th: ฉันติดใจทันที
เพื่อระบายความเครียด
ความรู้สึกภาคภูมิใจในความสำเร็จ
มันช่วยให้ใจฉันโล่ง

part2.title: Describe a goal you would like to achieve in the future
card: what the goal is
how long you have had this goal
what you need to do to achieve it
and explain why this goal is important to you
part2.model: A goal I'm really keen to achieve is to start my own small business, specifically a cosy little café that also sells books. I've had this dream for about four or five years now, ever since I spent a summer working part-time in a coffee shop and realised how much I enjoyed that kind of environment. To make it happen, there's quite a lot I still need to do. First and foremost, I need to save up a decent amount of capital, because rent in Bangkok isn't cheap at all. On top of that, I'd like to take a proper barista course and maybe some basic accounting classes, since I don't want to run into trouble with the finances later on. The reason this goal matters so much to me is that I've always wanted to create a space where people can slow down and relax, away from all the noise of the city. I'm not particularly interested in getting rich; I just want to build something that's genuinely mine. Hopefully, if everything goes to plan, I'll open the doors within the next five years.

phrases.th: ฉันอยากทำให้สำเร็จมากๆ
อย่างแรกและสำคัญที่สุด
ถ้าทุกอย่างเป็นไปตามแผน
บางสิ่งที่เป็นของฉันอย่างแท้จริง

part2.title: Describe a book you enjoyed reading
card: what the book was about
when you read it
why you decided to read it
and explain why you enjoyed it so much
part2.model: I'd like to talk about a novel called The Alchemist by Paulo Coelho, which I read a couple of years ago during a rather difficult period in my life. It's a fairly short book about a young shepherd who travels from Spain all the way to Egypt in search of treasure, but along the way he learns that the real treasure is the journey itself and following your dreams. I decided to pick it up because a close friend kept recommending it, and to be honest, I was a bit sceptical at first because it sounded a little too simple. However, once I started, I couldn't put it down. What I enjoyed most was how the story is written almost like a fable, so the language is beautifully simple yet the ideas are surprisingly deep. It really made me reflect on my own goals and whether I was chasing what I truly wanted. Even now, certain lines from the book pop into my head from time to time. All in all, it's the kind of book that stays with you long after you've finished it, and I've since recommended it to loads of people.

phrases.th: ฉันวางมันไม่ลงเลย

phrases.en: I was a bit sceptical at first
phrases.th: ตอนแรกฉันค่อนข้างลังเล
มันทำให้ฉันได้ทบทวนเรื่อง
มันติดตรึงอยู่กับคุณ

part2.title: Describe a skill you would like to learn
card: what the skill is
how you would learn it
how long you think it would take
and explain why you want to learn this skill
part2.model: A skill I've been dying to learn for ages is how to play the piano. I've always been drawn to music, but growing up my family couldn't really afford lessons or an instrument, so it just never happened. Now that I'm working and have a bit more freedom, I'd love to finally give it a go. As for how I'd learn, I'd probably start with online tutorials on YouTube, since there's an incredible amount of free material out there, and then once I'd got the basics down, I'd sign up for a proper teacher to correct my technique. Realistically, I think it would take me at least a couple of years of consistent practice before I could play anything I'd be proud of. The main reason I want to learn is that playing an instrument seems like such a wonderful way to unwind and express yourself. On top of that, I've read that learning music is great for your brain and even helps with memory. Honestly, whenever I watch someone play beautifully, I feel a little pang of regret that I never started. So this is something I'm determined to change.

phrases.th: ฉันอยากเรียนมันมากๆ

phrases.en: once I'd got the basics down
phrases.th: พอฉันจับพื้นฐานได้แล้ว
วิธีผ่อนคลายที่ยอดเยี่ยม
ความเสียดายเล็กๆ

part2.title: Describe a memorable meal you had
card: what the meal was
where and when you had it
who you were with
and explain why it was so memorable
part2.model: I'd like to describe a meal I had last year at a tiny family-run restaurant up in Chiang Mai. It was during a trip with a few of my old university friends, and we stumbled across the place completely by chance after getting lost looking for somewhere else. The restaurant only had about five tables and was run by an elderly couple, so everything felt wonderfully homemade. We ordered a whole spread of northern Thai dishes, including khao soi, which is this rich, creamy curry noodle soup that I still dream about. What made it so memorable wasn't just the food, although it was some of the best I've ever tasted. It was the whole atmosphere. We ended up sitting there for hours, chatting and laughing about old times, and the owners even came out to join us for a bit. To be honest, moments like that are becoming rarer now that everyone's so busy with their own lives. Looking back, it was one of those perfect evenings where everything just came together. Whenever I taste khao soi now, it takes me straight back to that night.

part2.title: Describe a film or TV show you would recommend

and explain why you would recommend it
part2.model: The show I'd definitely recommend is a series called Chef's Table, which is a documentary about world-famous chefs and the stories behind their cooking. I first watched it about a year ago when a colleague wouldn't stop raving about it, and I got completely absorbed. Each episode focuses on a different chef from somewhere around the world, and it dives into not just their food but their childhood, their failures, and what drives them. I'd recommend it to just about anyone, but especially to people who love food or who are feeling a bit stuck and need some inspiration. The reason I rate it so highly is that it's beautifully filmed; honestly, every shot looks like a work of art, and the storytelling is genuinely moving. More than once I found myself getting a little emotional, which I certainly wasn't expecting from a cooking show. On top of that, it left me feeling really motivated to pursue my own passions, whatever they might be. It's the sort of programme that entertains you and inspires you at the same time. I've since binge-watched every season, and I still go back to my favourite episodes.

part2.title: Describe a time you helped someone

part2.model: I'd like to talk about a time I helped an elderly neighbour of mine, an old lady who lives alone in the flat next to mine. It was during the rainy season last year, and I noticed that she hadn't collected her post for a few days, which was quite unusual for her. When I knocked on her door to check, it turned out she'd had a nasty fall and had hurt her ankle, so she could barely walk. Straight away, I offered to help. Over the following couple of weeks, I did her grocery shopping, picked up her medicine from the pharmacy, and just popped in every evening to make sure she was alright. It wasn't a huge amount of effort on my part, honestly, but it clearly made a big difference to her. As for how I felt, I was really glad I'd noticed in the first place, because she might have struggled on her own otherwise. It also reminded me how important it is to look out for the people around us, especially the elderly who don't have family nearby. All in all, it was a small thing that ended up meaning a lot.

part2.title: Describe a city you would like to visit

and explain why you want to visit it
part2.model: The city I'd absolutely love to visit one day is Kyoto in Japan. I first became fascinated with it through photos and travel videos online, and the more I saw, the more it drew me in. From what I understand, Kyoto is the cultural heart of Japan, full of ancient temples, traditional wooden houses, and beautiful gardens, which is a world away from the neon buzz of Tokyo. If I ever get the chance to go, there's so much I'd want to do. First of all, I'd love to wander through the famous bamboo forest at Arashiyama early in the morning before the crowds arrive. I'd also make a point of visiting the temples during autumn, when all the leaves turn red and gold. On top of that, I'm really keen to try an authentic tea ceremony, since I find that whole tradition fascinating. The main reason I want to go is that I've always been drawn to places that have managed to preserve their history and culture rather than modernising everything. Kyoto seems like the perfect blend of old and new, and I think it would be an unforgettable experience.

part2.title: Describe a teacher you remember

part2.model: I'd like to talk about Kru Wanida, my English teacher from secondary school in Khon Kaen. She taught me for three years, from Matthayom 4 to 6, so I got to know her really well. What made her stand out was that she never made us feel stupid for making mistakes. Back then, most teachers just drilled grammar from the textbook, but she brought in pop songs and movie clips, and she'd get us to actually speak, even if our English was pretty broken. I still remember her saying, 'A mistake out loud is worth more than a perfect sentence in your head.' Honestly, that stuck with me. She's the main reason I wasn't scared of English later on, at university and at work. We're still in touch, actually — I message her on Line every Teachers' Day. So yeah, I remember her because she completely changed my attitude towards learning. She turned something I dreaded into something I genuinely enjoy.

part2.model: I'm going to talk about my friend Ploy. We've known each other since primary school in Chiang Mai, so it's been over twenty years now, which is kind of amazing when I think about it. We met because we were seated next to each other in Prathom 3, and we just clicked straight away. These days she lives in Bangkok like me, and we try to meet up at least once a month for coffee or a proper meal. What I value most about her is that she's completely honest with me — if I'm about to make a bad decision, she'll tell me straight, but in a kind way. We've been through a lot together: exams, first jobs, a couple of messy breakups. Our friendship has lasted this long, I think, because we don't judge each other, and we make the effort to stay in touch even when life gets hectic. Honestly, she's more like a sister than a friend at this point.

part2.title: Describe a family member you admire

part2.model: The person I admire most in my family is my grandmother on my mum's side. She's in her late seventies now and still lives in our hometown in Nakhon Ratchasima. She raised five children pretty much on her own after my grandfather passed away young, and she did it by running a small noodle stall at the local market — she was up at four every morning for something like thirty years. What I really admire is that she never once complained. Even now, she's the calmest, most generous person I know; whenever relatives have problems, they go to her first. She's influenced me more than she probably realises. Whenever work gets stressful and I feel like giving up, I remind myself of what she went through, and my problems suddenly feel small. She also taught me to be careful with money but generous with people, which I try to live by. So yeah, if I turn out to be half as strong as her, I'll be pretty happy.

part2.title: Describe a famous person you would like to meet

part2.model: The famous person I'd really love to meet is Lisa from BLACKPINK. She's a K-pop star, but she's actually Thai — she grew up in Buriram, a fairly ordinary province in the northeast, before moving to Korea as a teenager to become a trainee. I first heard about her when BLACKPINK debuted, and I've followed her career ever since. What impresses me isn't just the singing and dancing, though she's obviously incredibly talented — it's how much she sacrificed. She left her family at fourteen, learned Korean from scratch, and trained for years with no guarantee of success. If I actually got to meet her, I'd want to ask how she coped with the pressure and the homesickness at such a young age. I think her answer would be genuinely useful for anyone chasing a big goal. And on top of that, she's put Thailand on the map internationally, which makes me quite proud. So yeah, she'd definitely be my pick.

phrases.en: I've followed her career ever since

part2.title: Describe a stranger who helped you

part2.model: I'd like to tell you about a stranger who helped me a couple of years ago. I was on my way to a really important job interview in Bangkok when my motorbike taxi got a flat tyre, and I was stranded on the side of Sukhumvit Road in the pouring rain. I was starting to panic because I had maybe twenty minutes left. Then a middle-aged office lady, a complete stranger, stopped under her umbrella and asked if I was okay. When I explained, she didn't hesitate — she flagged down a taxi for me, told the driver the fastest back route, and even offered to pay because I only had a large banknote. I made it with about five minutes to spare, and I actually got the job. Honestly, I was so relieved I could hardly speak. The thing is, I never got her name, so I couldn't thank her properly, which still bothers me a bit. That small act of kindness taught me that helping someone costs almost nothing, but it can honestly change their whole day — or in my case, my career.

part2.title: Describe a quiet place you like to go to relax

part2.model: The place I'd like to talk about is a small rooftop garden on top of my condo building in Bangkok. I discovered it maybe two years ago, and honestly, it's become my little escape. It's nothing fancy — just some potted plants, a couple of wooden benches, and a view over the neighbourhood — but because hardly anyone goes up there, it's incredibly peaceful. I go up there two or three evenings a week, usually just as the sun's going down, when the heat has eased off a bit. I'll take a cold drink, put my phone on silent, and just sit there watching the sky change colour. Sometimes I read, but mostly I do nothing at all, which is quite rare for me. What I love about it is the contrast — the city is so noisy and chaotic, and yet ten floors up you can barely hear the traffic. It gives me space to clear my head, and I always come back down feeling calmer and a bit more ready to face things.

part2.title: Describe a historical place you have visited
card: where this place is

part2.model: I'm going to talk about Ayutthaya, the old capital of Thailand, which is about an hour and a half from Bangkok by car. I went there last year with two friends because we all wanted a break from the city, and to be honest, I hadn't been back since a school trip years ago. We spent the whole day cycling around the historical park, visiting the ruined temples. The most famous spot is the Buddha head wrapped in tree roots at Wat Mahathat — you've probably seen photos of it. What struck me most was the scale of the place. Ayutthaya was one of the biggest cities in the world in its day, before it was destroyed in the eighteenth century, and you can still sense that from the size of the ruins. Standing among those crumbling brick towers, I felt this strange mix of pride and sadness. It made history feel real, not just something from a textbook. I'd genuinely recommend it to anyone visiting Thailand — it's well worth the trip.

part2.model: There's a little cafe near my office that's become my regular spot — a small independent coffee shop tucked down a side street, about five minutes' walk from where I work. I found it by accident about a year ago when I was trying to avoid the crowds at the big chain cafes, and now I go two or three times a week. It's run by a middle-aged couple who roast their own beans, and the smell when you walk in is just amazing. I usually order an iced latte and a slice of their coconut cake, which is homemade and honestly the best I've ever had. What keeps me going back, though, isn't just the food — it's the atmosphere. It's quiet enough to read or get some work done, they play soft jazz, and the owners remember my order, which makes the whole thing feel personal. In a big city, that kind of place is pretty rare. It's become part of my weekly routine, and I'd be genuinely sad if it ever closed down.

part2.model: I'd like to describe Lumpini Park, which is a big public park right in the centre of Bangkok, surrounded by office towers. I go there most weekends, usually early on Sunday morning before it gets too hot. It's got a large lake where you can rent paddle boats, wide paths for jogging, and these huge old trees that give plenty of shade. There are even monitor lizards wandering around, which shocks tourists, but locals are completely used to them. My usual routine is a slow jog around the lake, then I grab a fresh juice from one of the stalls and just people-watch for a while — there are groups doing tai chi, aerobics classes, older people playing chess. What I appreciate most is that it's a genuine green lung in the middle of a really dense, polluted city. The air honestly feels different in there, and it costs nothing. I think without that park my weekends would be a lot less healthy, so it plays a bigger role in my life than I sometimes realise.

part2.model: The shop I visit most often is a second-hand bookshop near my apartment — a small family-run place, absolutely crammed with books from floor to ceiling. I drop in maybe once a week, usually on my way home from work. I mainly go there to hunt for English novels, because reading in English is part of how I practise the language, and buying second-hand makes it affordable — most books cost less than a hundred baht. But honestly, half the pleasure is just browsing. There's no real system to the shelves, so you never know what you'll stumble across, and the owner, this lovely elderly man, always has recommendations if you ask. The shop has that musty smell of old paper, which I've genuinely grown to love. In an age when everyone shops online, I find something comforting about a physical shop where things happen slowly. It's partly nostalgia, I suppose, but every time I walk out with a good book for the price of a coffee, I feel like I've won a small prize.

part2.model: I'd like to talk about a gift I got from my younger sister about two years ago — a mechanical watch she gave me for my thirtieth birthday. To be honest, I wasn't expecting anything fancy, so when I opened the box I was genuinely speechless. It's got a dark blue face with a leather strap — nothing flashy, but really elegant. What makes it special is that she'd been saving up for months from her part-time job, which honestly means more to me than the watch itself. I wear it pretty much every day, especially to work meetings, and whenever I glance at it I'm reminded of her. It's also become a bit of a conversation starter — colleagues often ask where I got it. I suppose the reason it means so much is that it symbolises how close we've become as adults. We used to argue constantly as kids, so this gift felt like a milestone in our relationship, if that makes sense.

part2.model: The photo I want to describe is an old family picture taken at my grandparents' house in Chiang Mai when I was about seven. It shows the whole extended family — there must be nearly twenty of us — crammed together on the front steps during Songkran. My grandfather's right in the middle, laughing at something, and I'm sitting on his lap looking a bit grumpy because, apparently, I'd just been told off for splashing water indoors. My aunt found it in a shoebox a few years ago and had copies printed for everyone, and I keep mine on my desk at home. What makes it precious is that my grandfather passed away not long after it was taken, so it's actually one of the very few photos I have of him. Every time I look at it, all these little memories come flooding back — the smell of his cooking, the way he told stories. It's just a small, faded piece of paper, really, but honestly, it's one of my most treasured possessions.

part2.model: An app I find incredibly useful is Grab, which is basically the main ride-hailing and food delivery app here in Thailand. I first heard about it maybe five or six years ago when I moved to Bangkok for work — a colleague recommended it because I didn't have a car and taxis were a bit hit-and-miss. These days I use it almost daily — booking motorbike taxis to beat the traffic, ordering lunch to the office, even sending documents across town. What I love about it is how it takes the hassle out of everyday life. The fare's agreed upfront, so there's no haggling, and you can track your driver in real time. It's also saved me on countless rainy days when finding a cab would've been impossible. I suppose the downside is that I've become slightly too dependent on it — I probably order delivery more often than I should! But overall, it's genuinely changed how people in Thai cities get around, and I honestly can't imagine daily life without it now.

part2.model: The item I'd like to describe is a denim jacket that I've had for nearly ten years now. I bought it at Chatuchak weekend market when I was a university student — I remember bargaining the price down and feeling incredibly pleased with myself. It's a classic light-blue wash, slightly faded at the elbows now, with a small tear on one cuff that I've never bothered to fix. I mostly wear it in the cool season, or on planes and in cinemas where the air-conditioning is freezing. What I like about it is that it goes with literally everything — jeans, dresses, work clothes — so it's my safe choice whenever I can't decide what to wear. But beyond the practicality, it's sort of woven into my memories. I was wearing it on my first date with my partner and on my first trip abroad. Friends keep telling me to replace it because it's looking a bit shabby, but honestly, the worn-out look is exactly why I love it. It feels like an old friend.

part2.model: I'm going to talk about a wooden bookshelf I built myself during the pandemic, when we were all stuck at home with far too much free time. I'd been watching DIY videos on YouTube for weeks, and one day I just decided to give it a go. I ordered some pine boards online, borrowed a drill from my neighbour, and spent about three weekends measuring, sawing, and sanding on my balcony. To be honest, it didn't go smoothly at first — my first attempt was so wobbly that books slid straight off one shelf — so I had to take it apart and start again. The finished shelf isn't perfect; one side is very slightly crooked if you look closely. But it holds all my novels, and I stained it a lovely walnut colour that matches my room. I'm genuinely proud of it, mainly because I'm not a handy person at all. It taught me that with enough patience — and enough YouTube tutorials — you can figure out almost anything yourself.

part2.title: Describe a celebration you attended

part2.model: I'd like to talk about my grandmother's eightieth birthday party, which we held at my aunt's house in Khon Kaen about two years ago. It was a really big deal for our family because relatives flew in from all over — some cousins I hadn't seen in ages came all the way from Australia. In the morning we did a traditional merit-making ceremony with monks from the local temple, and then in the evening we had this huge dinner in the garden with about sixty guests. What made it special was a slideshow my cousin put together with old photos of my grandmother going back to the 1960s — honestly, there wasn't a dry eye in the house. My grandmother was absolutely over the moon; she kept saying it was the happiest day of her life. For me, the celebration mattered because it brought the whole family together under one roof, which hardly ever happens these days, and it reminded me how important it is to show the people you love how much they mean to you.

part2.title: Describe a journey that did not go as planned

part2.model: I'm going to tell you about a trip to Koh Chang that pretty much fell apart from the very beginning. My friend and I had booked a beachfront bungalow for a long weekend, and the plan was to drive down from Bangkok early on Friday morning. Well, first the car wouldn't start — the battery was completely dead — so we lost two hours waiting for a mechanic. Then, when we finally got to Trat, we missed the last car ferry by about ten minutes, which meant we had to spend the night in a slightly grim guesthouse on the mainland. To top it all off, it poured with rain the entire weekend, so all our beach plans went out the window. Funnily enough, though, it turned into one of my favourite trips. We ended up playing cards, eating amazing seafood at a little local place, and laughing about our terrible luck. It taught me that a trip doesn't have to go smoothly to be memorable — sometimes the disasters make the best stories.

part2.title: Describe a difficult decision you made
card: what the decision was

part2.model: The hardest decision I've ever made was resigning from a secure office job to go back to studying. I'd been working in accounting for almost five years, and on paper everything was fine — steady salary, decent benefits — but deep down I knew my heart wasn't in it. What I really wanted was to work in software, so I had to decide whether to quit and take an intensive coding course, or play it safe and study part-time at weekends instead. It was a real dilemma because I had savings to think about, and my parents were worried I was throwing away a good career. I weighed up the pros and cons for months — I even made a spreadsheet! In the end, I told myself that staying somewhere just because it's comfortable is its own kind of risk. Looking back, it was absolutely the right call. The first year was tough financially, but I now do work that I genuinely enjoy. That decision taught me that big choices are scary precisely because they matter — and that's usually a sign you should face them, not avoid them.

part2.title: Describe a success you are proud of

part2.model: A success I'm genuinely proud of is completing my first half marathon in Bangkok about eighteen months ago. To be honest, I was never a sporty person — before I started training, I couldn't even run for ten minutes without stopping. But after a health check-up gave me a bit of a wake-up call, I decided to sign up and commit to a four-month training plan. It was far from easy. I had to get up at five in the morning to run before work, and there were plenty of days when I wanted to throw in the towel, especially during the rainy season. On race day, the last five kilometres were absolute agony, but crossing that finish line was one of the best feelings of my life. I'm proud of it not because of my time — which was pretty average, to be fair — but because I proved to myself that I can stick with something difficult. It completely changed how I see myself, and I've kept running ever since.

part2.model: I'd like to describe a lovely surprise I got on my birthday a couple of years ago. I was living away from my hometown for work, and I'd assumed I'd be spending my birthday alone — probably just ordering some food and watching a series. Then, around seven in the evening, the doorbell rang, and when I opened the door, my two best friends from university were standing there holding a cake and a bag of my favourite snacks. It turned out they'd been planning it for weeks — they'd taken the train up from Bangkok and even coordinated with my flatmate to make sure I'd be home. I was completely speechless; I actually teared up a little, which is quite unlike me. We stayed up half the night catching up and reminiscing about our university days. What made it so special was the effort behind it — it wasn't an expensive gift, it was their time. That evening reminded me that the people who show up for you are the ones worth holding on to.

part2.model: I'd like to talk about the time I learned to swim, which was actually only about two years ago — quite embarrassing, since most people learn as kids. I signed up for an adult swimming class at a pool near my office in Bangkok because a friend convinced me to join her. Honestly, the first few lessons were terrifying. I couldn't even put my face in the water without panicking, and I felt pretty silly next to the seven-year-olds in the next lane. But the instructor was incredibly patient — she broke everything down into tiny steps, like just blowing bubbles at first. After about two months of practising twice a week, something suddenly clicked, and I managed my first full lap of the pool. I actually got a bit emotional, to be honest. It taught me that it's never too late to pick up a new skill, and now swimming has become my favourite way to unwind after a long day at work.

part2.model: I'm going to tell you about the time I was late for a job interview, which still makes me cringe when I think about it. This was about three years ago, and I'd been shortlisted for a marketing position I really wanted. I planned to leave home early, but that morning there was a sudden downpour and the roads in Bangkok were completely gridlocked. My taxi barely moved for forty minutes, so I jumped out and ran to the nearest BTS station, absolutely drenched. By the time I arrived, I was twenty-five minutes late and looked like I'd swum there. I apologised profusely and explained the situation, and luckily the interviewer was quite understanding — apparently half the candidates were stuck in the same storm. Amazingly, I ended up getting the job anyway. Still, the whole experience taught me a valuable lesson: in a city like Bangkok, you can never leave too early. These days I always build in at least an hour of buffer time for anything important.

part2.title: Describe a time you experienced extreme weather

part2.model: I'd like to describe the massive flood that hit my hometown a few years back — definitely the most extreme weather I've ever experienced. It had been raining non-stop for almost a week, and one morning we woke up to find water creeping into the ground floor of our house. Within a day it was knee-deep. We rushed to move the furniture and electrical appliances upstairs, and my dad stacked sandbags at the front door, though honestly they didn't help much. For nearly two weeks we were basically stranded — the whole neighbourhood got around by boat, and volunteers paddled past delivering drinking water and instant noodles. What struck me most was how the community pulled together; neighbours who barely spoke before were suddenly sharing food and checking on the elderly. When the water finally receded, the clean-up was exhausting, and we lost quite a few belongings. But the experience made me far more aware of climate change, and to this day I keep an emergency kit ready every rainy season.

part2.model: I still remember my first day at my current company vividly, even though it was over four years ago. It was a digital marketing agency in central Bangkok, and I was fresh out of university, so I was a bundle of nerves. I remember changing my outfit three times that morning and arriving ridiculously early — like forty minutes before anyone else. When people finally showed up, my manager gave me a tour of the office and introduced me to the team, and I was terrified I'd forget everyone's names — which, of course, I did. The best part was that my mentor, a senior colleague named Ple, took me out for lunch with a few teammates, and they shared funny stories about their own first-day disasters, which really put me at ease. By the end of the day, my head was spinning with new information, but I went home feeling excited rather than anxious. Looking back, that warm welcome shaped how I now treat every newcomer who joins our team.

part2.model: I'd like to talk about the time I lost my phone in a taxi, which was honestly one of the most stressful evenings of my life. It happened about a year ago after a late dinner with friends. I must have dozed off in the back seat, and when I got home I realised my pocket was empty. My heart just sank — everything was on that phone: photos, banking apps, work contacts, the lot. I immediately borrowed my sister's phone to call my number, but it went straight to voicemail, which made me fear the worst. Then I remembered I'd booked the ride through an app, so I contacted the driver through the platform. To my huge relief, he answered and said he'd found it wedged between the seats, and he kindly drove all the way back to return it — I gave him a generous tip, of course. The whole ordeal only lasted about two hours, but it felt like forever. Since then, I've backed everything up to the cloud, and I always double-check my seat before getting out.

The lyrics are about putting in effort and still falling short, which honestly felt like it was written about my life at the time. What makes it so special to me is the memory attached to it. Whenever it comes on, I'm instantly taken back to that period, and I remember that I got through it. It's become a kind of comfort song for me — if I'm feeling down or overwhelmed, I put on my headphones and play it, and it never fails to lift my spirits. I guess some songs just stay with you for life.

part2.model: The ad I remember best is a Thai life insurance commercial that went viral maybe eight or nine years ago. I first saw it on TV, but it spread everywhere online — even people overseas were sharing it. It didn't feel like an advertisement at all, more like a short film. It followed an ordinary man who did small acts of kindness every day — watering a dying plant, giving money to a young girl on the street, feeding a stray dog — and nobody noticed or thanked him. Then at the end, you see the plant blooming and the girl going to school, and you realise his kindness quietly changed lives. Only in the last few seconds did the company logo appear. Honestly, it brought tears to my eyes the first time, and I'm not usually emotional about ads. I think it stuck in my memory because it sold a feeling rather than a product. Most commercials shout at you to buy something, but this one just told a beautiful story, and to me that's far more powerful.

It's about a robot cat from the future who helps a clumsy schoolboy called Nobita using magical gadgets from his pocket. I must have watched it religiously from about age six to twelve. My younger cousin and I would wake up early, grab some snacks, and sit glued to the screen for the whole morning — it was basically our weekend ritual. Looking back, I think I loved it for two reasons. First, the gadgets — the 'Anywhere Door' that could take you anywhere in the world, the bamboo copter you could fly with. As a kid, I genuinely dreamed of owning them. And second, watching it with my cousin made it special; we'd act out the episodes afterwards and argue about which gadget was the best. Even now, when I hear the theme song somewhere, I get this warm wave of nostalgia. It really was a huge part of my childhood.

part2.model: The good news I'd like to share is that my younger sister got accepted into the nursing programme she'd been dreaming about for years. I heard it maybe two months ago — she called me while I was at work, and she was so excited she could barely get the words out. I had to ask her to slow down and say it again. To give you some background, she'd applied the year before and didn't get in, which really knocked her confidence. But instead of giving up, she spent a whole year preparing — retaking exams and volunteering at a local hospital to strengthen her application. So this wasn't luck at all; she thoroughly deserved it. When she finally said 'I got in,' I honestly got goosebumps. I think I might've been even happier than she was. That evening the whole family went out for dinner to celebrate, and my mum was in tears. It reminded me that persistence really does pay off, and it's easily the best news our family has had in a long time.

part2.title: Describe a photograph you took that you like
card: what the photo shows
when and where you took it
why you took it
and explain why you like it so much
part2.model: I'd like to describe a photo I took of my grandmother at her eightieth birthday party, about two years ago. We held a small celebration at her house in the countryside — just family, nothing fancy. The photo shows her laughing, really laughing, with her head back and her eyes almost closed, because my little nephew had just smeared birthday cake all over his own face. I wasn't planning to take a picture at that exact moment; I just happened to have my phone out and caught it by pure chance. What I love about it is how natural it is. My grandmother usually goes stiff and serious the second a camera points at her, so getting a candid shot of her genuinely happy is really rare. The lighting was lovely too — soft afternoon sun coming through the window. I've since printed and framed it, and it sits on my desk at work. Whenever I'm having a rough day, I glance at it and it instantly cheers me up. Of the thousands of photos on my phone, that's the one I treasure most.

part2.title: Describe a change you would like to make in your life
card: what change you want to make

part2.model: Okay, so the change I really want to make is cutting down on my screen time, especially at night. To be honest, I've got into this terrible habit of scrolling through my phone in bed, sometimes until one in the morning, and it's really affecting my sleep. I started noticing it about a year ago, when I realised I was feeling exhausted at work almost every day, even though technically I was in bed for eight hours. What I plan to do is leave my phone charging in the living room and read a paper book instead — that's something my sister does, and she swears by it. It won't be easy, because checking my phone has become almost automatic, like a reflex. But I think the benefits would be huge: better sleep, more energy, and honestly, more time for things that actually matter, like talking to my family properly. So yeah, that's the change I'm determined to make this year — hopefully starting tonight, actually!

part2.title: Describe a place where you would like to live in the future
card: where this place is

part2.model: The place I'd love to live in the future is Chiang Mai, up in the north of Thailand. I'm from Bangkok, and don't get me wrong, I love the energy of the capital, but the traffic and the pace of life can be pretty draining. I've visited Chiang Mai maybe five or six times on holiday, and every single time I come back feeling recharged. What appeals to me is the combination of things — it's surrounded by mountains, the cost of living is lower, and there's this lovely café culture where people actually take their time. Ideally, I'd move there in my late thirties, once I can work remotely or set up a small business, maybe a little guesthouse. Of course, it's not perfect — the burning season around March brings terrible air pollution, so I'd probably escape down south for a month each year. But overall, I picture myself having a slower, healthier lifestyle there, and that idea genuinely makes me happy.

part2.title: Describe something you would like to buy in the future
card: what you would like to buy

part2.model: Something I've been saving up for is an electric car — ideally one of those compact EVs that have become really popular in Thailand recently. At the moment I drive a ten-year-old petrol car that my dad passed down to me, and while it still runs fine, the fuel costs are eating into my salary, especially with the amount of driving I do for work. I've wanted one for about two years now, and I've done quite a lot of research — comparing models, reading reviews, even test-driving a couple at the motor show. I reckon I'll need about one more year of putting money aside each month to cover the down payment. The main reason is practical: charging at home would cost a fraction of what I spend on petrol. But I'll admit there's an environmental side too — Bangkok's air quality is awful, and I'd like to feel I'm part of the solution rather than the problem. So fingers crossed, by this time next year I'll be driving one.

part2.title: Describe a subject you would like to study

part2.model: A subject I'd really like to study properly is psychology — specifically how people make decisions. My interest actually started at work, funnily enough. I'm in marketing, and I kept noticing that customers don't behave the way logic says they should — they'll happily pay more for something just because of how it's presented. That got me curious, so I started listening to podcasts and reading books like 'Thinking, Fast and Slow', but I feel like I've only scratched the surface. What I'd love to do is take a proper online course, maybe a part-time diploma from a university, where I'd have real assignments and feedback rather than just passively consuming content. Realistically, I could study in the evenings and at weekends, though juggling it with a full-time job would definitely be a challenge. I think it would benefit me in two ways: professionally, it would make me much better at my job, and personally, I'd just understand people — including myself — a whole lot better.

part2.model: If I got the chance, the country I'd most like to work in is Japan, probably in Osaka or Tokyo. I've been fascinated by Japan since I was a teenager — first through anime, I'll admit — but these days it's more about their working culture around craftsmanship, this idea that whatever you do, you do it properly. Career-wise, I work in software, and Japan has a real shortage of IT professionals, so companies there actively recruit from Southeast Asia, and the salaries are noticeably higher than in Thailand. I'd probably aim to go for two or three years rather than permanently — long enough to gain solid experience and save some money, but I'd want to come back to be near my parents eventually. The biggest hurdle is obviously the language; my Japanese is still very basic, so I've started taking lessons twice a week. It would push me right out of my comfort zone, but I honestly believe working abroad changes you in ways that travel never can.

part2.title: Describe a healthy habit you have

part2.model: I'd like to talk about a habit I picked up about two years ago, which is going for a walk every morning before work. I usually head out around six, when the air's still cool, and do a loop around the park near my condo — it takes maybe forty minutes. Honestly, I started it for a pretty unglamorous reason: I'd been sitting at a desk all day and my back was killing me, so my doctor suggested light exercise. What surprised me was how much it helped my mood, not just my body. There's something about walking while the city's still quiet that clears my head — I often sort out problems from work without even trying. These days, if I skip a morning for some reason, I actually feel a bit off for the rest of the day. It's become non-negotiable for me. I'd say it's the single best thing I've done for my health, and it costs absolutely nothing, which is a nice bonus.

part2.model: The app I use every single day, without fail, is YouTube. I check it first thing with my coffee and usually again in the evening while I'm having dinner. What I mainly watch is a mix of English-learning channels, cooking videos, and these long documentary-style videos about how businesses work. I think what keeps me coming back is that it feels tailored to me — the recommendations know exactly what I'm into, sometimes a bit too well, to be honest. It's also genuinely useful, not just entertainment. When my washing machine broke last month, I fixed it myself by following a ten-minute tutorial, which saved me a decent amount of money. Of course, there's a downside — it's incredibly easy to fall down a rabbit hole and lose an hour without noticing, so I've started setting a timer for myself. But overall, I'd say it's the one app I couldn't realistically give up. It's basically replaced television for me, and honestly, half of my general knowledge probably comes from it.

part2.model: The person I probably talk to most often, apart from my colleagues, is my older sister, Nan. She's four years older than me and works as a nurse in another province, so we don't see each other much in person, but we call each other almost every evening, usually while I'm walking home. Our conversations are nothing special on the surface — we talk about what we ate, annoying things that happened at work, our parents, that kind of thing. But she's also the first person I go to when something's genuinely bothering me. She has this way of listening without immediately jumping in with advice, which I really appreciate, because most people just tell you what to do. Growing up we actually fought constantly, so it's funny that we've ended up so close as adults. I think talking to her keeps me grounded — she knew me before I was anybody's employee or anybody's colleague. Honestly, a week without one of our calls would feel strange and incomplete to me.

part2.model: I'd like to describe my evening cooking routine, which has honestly become the best part of my day. Around seven, once I'm home from work, I put on a playlist — usually old Thai pop or city pop — pour myself a glass of something cold, and start cooking dinner from scratch. It doesn't have to be fancy; often it's just a simple stir-fry or an omelette over rice. I think what I love about it is that it's the one part of my day where I'm not staring at a screen. Chopping vegetables is weirdly meditative — my hands are busy, but my mind gets to wander. It started during the pandemic, when eating out wasn't an option, and it just stuck. Before that, I used to grab street food on the way home almost every night. Now, even when I'm exhausted, I still look forward to that hour. It marks a clear line between work mode and home mode, and I genuinely think I'd be a more stressed person without it.

part2.title: Describe a piece of advice you received
card: what the advice was

part2.model: The advice I want to talk about came from my first boss, back when I'd just graduated and started my first office job. I was the kind of person who would polish a report endlessly and miss deadlines because nothing ever felt good enough. One day she pulled me aside and said, quite bluntly, 'Done is better than perfect.' At the time I was a bit offended, to be honest — I thought she was telling me to lower my standards. But the more I thought about it, the more it made sense. She wasn't saying quality doesn't matter; she was saying that an imperfect thing that actually exists beats a perfect thing that never gets finished. That one sentence has genuinely changed how I work. Whenever I catch myself procrastinating or endlessly tweaking something, I hear her voice in my head and just submit the thing. I've even passed it on to junior colleagues now. It's funny how a single offhand comment can end up shaping your whole working life.
